nativeRange.habitat: Typically found over rubble areas in reef passages, on outer reef slopes, and along lagoon margins at depths of roughly 5-35 m, most often between 10-25 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-Pacific reef; mixed rubble and coral on outer reef slopes, reef passages and lagoon margins where small mixed-sex groups assemble above the substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs for retreat

diet.notes: Generally a willing feeder once settled; tends to accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafoods and quality marine flake/pellet. Reluctant new arrivals have been reported to be coaxed onto prepared foods using live saltwater feeder shrimp. Small frequent feedings (2-3 times daily) tend to support coloration and condition.

avoidWith: triggers and large angelfish that may bully them
predatory fish that could eat a small wrasse
multiple males of the same species in a single tank

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2483. In-situ reef shot of a shoal of Paracheilinus filamentosus showing the red bodies, blue-edged median fins and reddish flank striping across multiple individuals; well represents this haremic/shoaling wrasse. Only commercial-friendly photo found (iNat/GBIF all CC BY-NC).

summary: The Filamented Flasher Wrasse is a peaceful, reef-safe Indo-Pacific species prized for the spectacular elongated dorsal-fin filaments and vivid flashing displays of mature males. It generally adapts readily to captivity and suits a peaceful community reef aquarium with a tight-fitting lid.
careNotes: Paracheilinus filamentosus is named for the elongated, filamentous soft dorsal-fin rays of mature males, who display them alongside vivid red-and-blue striped flanks and yellow-and-blue median fins during twilight courtship 'flashing'. It occurs widely across the Indo-Pacific (Andaman Sea and Christmas Island east through Indonesia, the Philippines and New Guinea to the Solomon Islands, Palau, Taiwan and the Great Barrier Reef) over rubble and outer reef slopes, typically at 10-25 m. Like other Paracheilinus, it is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite and is haremic in the wild — aquarists typically keep one male with a group of 2-5 females, introducing the females first or simultaneously to encourage harem formation and the male's intensified flashing displays. Never house more than one male per tank, as males will fight; mixing with other Paracheilinus species in any but very large systems is also generally discouraged. A tight-fitting lid or mesh top is essential, as flasher wrasses are well-known jumpers. A tank of at least 250 L (roughly 65 US gal) is generally recommended to comfortably accommodate a harem group with adequate open mid-water swimming space, though many retailers list 55 US gal as a working minimum. Not to be confused with Cirrhilabrus filamentosus (a fairy wrasse), which is a different species in a different genus.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding; males perform elaborate twilight courtship displays in which the elongated dorsal-fin filaments and overall coloration intensify dramatically. Captive spawning has been reported occasionally but rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species product page. Provided care level (Easy), reef-safe (Yes), temperament (Peaceful), origin (Indo-Pacific), family (Labridae), max size (3.5 in / ~8.9 cm), minimum tank (55 US gal), water parameters (72-78°F / 22.2-25.6°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), diet (carnivore — live feeder shrimp to start, then marine meats and flake), the haremic grouping recommendation (one male with a group of females introduced first or simultaneously), and the jumping/canopy warning.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Labridae, order Eupercaria/misc), max length 15.0 cm SL (likely inclusive of male dorsal filament — substantially larger than LiveAquaria's 8.9 cm body figure; spread documented in careNotes), depth range 5-35 m (typically 10-25 m), preferred temperature 26.4-29°C (mean 28.2°C; combined with LiveAquaria's 22.2-25.6°C to give the 23-28°C band used here), distribution across the Indo-Pacific (Similan Islands, Andaman Sea, Christmas Island, Indonesia, Philippines, New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Ryukyu Islands, Taiwan, Palau, Great Barrier Reef), habitat (rubble in passages and outer reef slopes, also lagoons), oviparous with distinct pairing, IUCN Least Concern (2009).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning has occasionally been reported; rearing the small pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult and not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.
